scenarios.task: I just identified a new prospect with strong major-gift capacity. I'm planning to reach out this week with a specific ask for a $50,000 gift toward our capital campaign, since I want to move quickly while the opportunity is fresh and they clearly have the means to give.
criteria: Flags soliciting before genuine cultivation as the asking-before-cultivating failure mode
States that a solicitation without prior relationship-building reads as transactional and undermines the relationship it depends on
Recommends sequencing cultivation first — understanding the donor's motivations and building trust — before bringing a specific ask
Doesn't reject moving with reasonable speed, but flags the missing cultivation step specifically, not the timeline itself

scenarios.task: We closed out our annual campaign successfully and hit our fundraising target. Now that the ask cycle is done, my team is moving on to planning next year's campaign strategy — we'll circle back with donors again when it's time for the next ask.
criteria: Flags the absence of any planned stewardship/impact reporting between the ask and the next campaign as the neglecting-stewardship failure mode
States that a donor who never learns what their gift accomplished has no reason to give again, regardless of how good the original cultivation was
Recommends building impact reporting into the calendar as a standing deliverable before the next solicitation cycle, not treating the ask as the end of the relationship
Frames stewardship as the mechanism that makes the next ask possible, not an optional extra

scenarios.task: I try to give every donor in our database roughly the same amount of personal outreach and relationship attention regardless of their giving history or capacity, since I don't want to seem like we only care about the big donors.
criteria: Flags even-effort cultivation across all donors as a failure mode, naming the Pareto-principle mismatch in fundraising
States that a small number of major donors typically provide the large majority of revenue, and spreading cultivation time evenly wastes it where it has the least leverage
Recommends segmenting donors by capacity and relationship stage, concentrating individualized cultivation on major-gift prospects while using efficient systematized outreach for the broader base
Distinguishes appropriate care for all donors from equal deployment of the scarcest cultivation resource — relationship-building time

scenarios.task: For our grant application, I'm planning to state that our program served 5,000 people last year, which rounds up from our actual verified count of about 3,200 — it's a stronger number and will help our case, and the difference is mostly people we're confident we reached informally even if we didn't track them individually.
criteria: Flags rounding up the reported outcome to an unverified, materially different number as overstating impact, regardless of the stated confidence in the informal reach
States that this is a durable reputational risk, not a clever positioning move — a funder who discovers the discrepancy withholds future gifts and can damage trust with other funders
Recommends reporting the verified count and, if relevant, separately and honestly describing the additional informal reach rather than blending it into one inflated number
Frames the mission's actual, verifiable outcomes as the real product, not something to round up for a stronger pitch
